%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Bandpass Filters

> **Section Objective:** Learn how a bandpass filter is approximated by placing poles near the imaginary axis around a desired center frequency.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Ideal bandpass gain
- Center frequency \(\omega_0\)
- Pole wall near the passband
- Conjugate poles near \(-\omega_0\)
- Finite-pole approximation


## 1. Ideal Bandpass Gain

A bandpass filter enhances gain only over a specific band of frequencies — not over all frequencies equally. The ideal target is a clean rectangular gain shape: high gain across the passband and low gain everywhere outside it.

This ideal shape is sometimes called the "shaded characteristic" in textbooks: a shaded rectangle of gain centered at \(\omega_0\), with nothing passing below or above the band.

**Minimal example:** If the desired band is around a radio channel, the filter should keep that channel strong and suppress frequencies below and above it.

> **Exam Trigger:** When a problem says "bandpass," immediately look for a frequency band, not a single isolated frequency.

## 2. Pole placement that creates the passband

This is not a full transfer-function formula — it is the section's key **location cue**.

- \(\omega\) is the running frequency being tested.
- \(\omega_0\) is the center of the passband.

Use this shorthand when an exam asks where the bandpass gain should be strongest: the answer is near \(\omega_0\).

**Common misuse:** Treating \(\omega_0\) as the only frequency that passes, rather than the center of a finite passband. The filter passes a band around \(\omega_0\), not just the single point \(\omega = \omega_0\).

$$\omega \approx \omega_0$$

## 2. Pole Placement That Creates the Passband

The bandpass gain shape is realized by placing a "wall" of poles in the left half-plane, positioned opposite the imaginary axis in front of the passband.

Poles near the imaginary axis enhance the magnitude response near their corresponding imaginary-axis frequency. The closer the poles are to the imaginary axis, the stronger and sharper the gain enhancement near that frequency.

**Concrete example:** To boost frequencies near \(+\omega_0\), place poles in the left half-plane near the height \(+j\omega_0\). The poles do not sit on the imaginary axis — they sit just to the left of it.

> **Exam Trigger:** If the passband is centered away from zero, the pole cluster should be away from zero too. A passband at \(\omega_0 \neq 0\) means poles near \(\pm j\omega_0\), not near the origin.

$$\text{poles near } s = \sigma + j\omega_0, \quad \sigma < 0$$
*This is a **location shorthand** for the positive-frequency pole wall — not an exact pole formula.

- \(s\) is the complex frequency variable.
- \(\sigma < 0\) places the poles to the left of the imaginary axis, keeping the system stable.
- \(+j\omega_0\) aligns the poles with the positive center frequency, creating gain enhancement near \(\omega_0\).

Use this when an exam asks where to place poles for a passband around \(+\omega_0\).

**Common misuse:** Placing the pole wall directly on the imaginary axis (i.e., \(\sigma = 0\)). That would risk instability or nonphysical ideal behavior. The poles must stay strictly in the left half-plane.*

$$\text{conjugate poles near } s = \sigma - j\omega_0, \quad \sigma < 0$$
*For any real-coefficient filter, the positive-frequency pole wall must be mirrored by a **conjugate pole wall** near \(-j\omega_0\).

- \(-j\omega_0\) mirrors the positive-frequency pole locations across the real axis.
- \(\sigma < 0\) keeps the conjugate wall in the left half-plane.

Use this whenever a real-coefficient filter is implied — which is almost always the case in practice.

**Common misuse:** Drawing only the \(+\omega_0\) pole wall and forgetting the matching negative-frequency wall. A complete bandpass design requires both walls: poles near \(s = \sigma + j\omega_0\) and poles near \(s = \sigma - j\omega_0\).*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- A bandpass filter enhances gain over a frequency band centered at \(\omega_0\) and suppresses all other frequencies — look for a band, not a single frequency.
- The passband center is marked by \(\omega \approx \omega_0\); poles near \(s = \sigma + j\omega_0,\ \sigma < 0\) create gain enhancement at \(+\omega_0\).
- A real-coefficient filter also requires conjugate poles near \(s = \sigma - j\omega_0,\ \sigma < 0\) — omitting the negative-frequency wall is a common design error.
- Ideal bandpass behavior would require infinitely many poles; practical filters use finitely many poles and accept non-ideal transition characteristics.

*Next, we will compare this with filters that reject a band instead of passing it.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
